title: Ciabatta
subtitle: High-hydration Italian loaf with an open, irregular crumb
excerpt: Ciabatta is defined by its wet dough and the open crumb that comes from handling it as little as possible. Stretch-and-folds replace kneading; shaping is minimal. The irregular holes are not a sign of underproofing — they are the point.
sourceNotes: Ciabatta was developed in 1982 by Arnaldo Cavallari of Adria, Veneto, as an Italian response to the French baguette, and quickly became widely documented in Italian and international domestic cookery. The wet-dough, minimal-handling technique is standard across all published forms.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice and freeze. Toast from frozen or warm in a 180°C oven for 8 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cold-ferment overnight after bulk: cover the bowl and refrigerate. Shape directly from the cold dough the next day.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dough strengthening technique used in place of kneading for high-hydration doughs. The dough is grabbed at one edge, stretched upward until it resists, then folded back over itself. Repeated on all four sides, this is one set. Performed in sets spaced 30 minutes apart during bulk fermentation, it builds gluten strength without tearing the dough or degassing the fermentation.

content.text: Ciabatta dough is wetter than almost anything else you will make. It should be handled with wet hands, not dry ones, and moved with a bench scraper rather than lifted by hand. The open crumb is produced by
sets during bulk fermentation, by minimal degassing during shaping, and by baking in a very hot oven with steam in the first few minutes.
The bench scraper is not optional here. Use it to flip the dough, to divide it, and to scoop the shaped loaves onto the baking paper. Trying to lift this dough with your hands will deflate it.
Mix and bulk ferment with stretch-and-folds, 2 hours

Combine {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, and {{salt-table}} in a large bowl. Add {{water}} and {{olive-oil}}. Mix with your hands or a dough scraper until no dry flour remains — the dough will be extremely wet and sticky. Cover and rest 30 minutes. Perform a set of stretch-and-folds: reach under the dough with a wet hand, pull one edge up and fold it over. Repeat on all four sides. Cover and rest 30 minutes. Repeat the stretch-and-fold set two more times at 30-minute intervals. After the final fold the dough should hold some shape and feel stronger.

Flour a work surface generously. Tip the dough out without punching it down. Flour the top surface and use the bench scraper to divide into two equal rectangles. Use the scraper to gently stretch each piece slightly lengthwise, then flip it onto a floured sheet of baking paper. Don't overhandle. Leave uncovered for 45 minutes — ciabatta proves flat, not in tins. Heat the oven to 230°C (210°C fan) with a roasting tin on the bottom shelf.

Slide the loaves on their paper onto a hot baking tray. Pour a cup of boiling water into the roasting tin on the bottom shelf and immediately close the oven door. Bake for 20–22 minutes until deep gold with a firm, hollow-sounding base. The crust will be hard when it comes out but soften after 20 minutes on a wire rack.
